votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the length check (handling the nat subtraction property where 0-1=0) and the element-wise derivative relationship defined in the Coq specification.
The Python code correctly checks the length and the elements of the result list according to the Coq specification.
The Python code correctly checks the length and the elements of the result list according to the Coq specification.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec: it checks that the result length is len(xs)-1 (using max(0,...) to handle edge cases), and for each index i, verifies that result[i] equals xs[i+1] * (i+1). This matches the Coq spec where nth i result 0 = nth (S i) xs 0 * Z.of_nat (S i), since S i in Coq corresponds to i+1 in Python (0-indexed), and Z.of_nat (S i) equals i+1.

The Python code correctly checks that the result has length max(0, len(xs)-1), and for each index i in range(len(result)), it verifies result[i] == xs[i+1] * (i+1). This matches the Coq spec which requires length result = (length xs - 1) and nth i result 0 = nth (S i) xs 0 * Z.of_nat (S i) for all i < length result. The indexing (i+1 for xs, i+1 as multiplier) and length conditions are equivalent.

The Python code correctly checks that the result length is len(xs) - 1 (using max(0, ...) to handle edge cases), and for each index i in result, it verifies result[i] == xs[i+1] * (i+1). This matches the Coq spec where nth i result 0 = nth (S i) xs 0 * Z.of_nat (S i), since S i in Coq corresponds to i+1 in Python (0-indexed), and Z.of_nat (S i) equals i+1 for 0-indexed i.
Both require result length to be max(0, len(xs)-1) and for each i < len(result), result[i] equals xs[i+1]*(i+1); indices are in-bounds so Coq defaults are irrelevant and Python integers match Z.
Both require len(result) = max(0, len(xs)-1) and for each i < len(result), result[i] == xs[i+1] * (i+1); nth defaults are never used under the bound, so behavior matches.
Length check uses nat-style subtraction via max(0, len(xs)-1), and for each i it verifies result[i] = xs[i+1]*(i+1); indices are in-bounds so defaults aren’t used. Concrete behavior matches.
